json.token-count.seo.article:
Token Counter - Count LLM Model Token Quantities

What is a Token?

Token is the basic unit that Large Language Models (LLMs) use to process text, also called a word piece. Unlike simple character counting or word counting, a Token can be a complete word, part of a word, a punctuation mark, a number, or even a single character. Different LLMs use different Tokenizers, so the same text may have different Token quantities in different models. For example, "hello" is one Token in some models, but may be split into two Tokens ("hel" and "lo") in others; "你好" is split into multiple Tokens in most models (usually each Chinese character takes 2-3 Tokens), but may be one Token in models supporting Chinese.

Understanding Token quantity is very important for three reasons: First, most LLM APIs (like OpenAI GPT-4, Anthropic Claude, Google Gemini, Azure OpenAI, Alibaba Cloud Tongyi Qianwen, etc.) charge by Token, accurately estimating Token quantity can predict and control API call costs, avoiding unexpected high fees; Second, LLMs have Context Window limits, the maximum Token quantity a model can process at once (like GPT-4 is 128K Tokens, Claude 3 is 200K Tokens), if input or output Token quantity exceeds the limit, the API call will fail or be truncated; Third, when processing long text, need to reasonably segment to ensure each segment is within the model's capability range, Token counting helps optimize segmentation strategies.

Token granularity affects model performance and effectiveness: coarse-grained Tokens (each Token contains more characters) can reduce sequence length, increase processing speed, but may reduce model understanding ability; fine-grained Tokens (each Token contains fewer characters) can increase sequence length, reduce processing speed, but may improve model understanding ability, especially for rare vocabulary and multilingual text. Modern LLMs typically use a compromise Tokenizer, like GPT-4 uses a BPE (Byte-Pair Encoding) variant, balancing performance and understanding ability.

Why Do You Need Token Counting?

Cost Control: Most LLM providers charge by Token usage (both input and output). For example, OpenAI GPT-4 charges about $0.03 per 1K input Tokens and $0.06 per 1K output Tokens; Anthropic Claude charges about $0.003 per 1K input Tokens and $0.015 per 1K output Tokens. If you don't know Token quantity, you can't predict API call costs. By using Token counting tools, you can estimate costs before calling APIs, avoid unexpected high bills, and optimize your prompt to reduce Token usage.

Context Window Management: Each LLM model has a Context Window limit, which is the maximum Token quantity the model can process at once. For example, GPT-3.5-turbo has a Context Window of 4K or 16K Tokens, GPT-4 has 8K/32K/128K Tokens, Claude 3 has 200K Tokens. If your input or output exceeds this limit, the API call will fail or text will be truncated. By counting Tokens, you can determine whether text needs segmentation, or whether you need to use a model with a larger Context Window.

Prompt Optimization: When writing prompts for LLMs, you need to balance detail and Token consumption. Longer prompts may provide more context and instructions, but also consume more Tokens and increase costs. By counting Tokens, you can optimize prompts to remove redundant content while retaining necessary information, achieving the best results at the lowest cost.

Model Comparison: Different LLM models use different Tokenizers, so the same text has different Token quantities in different models. By counting Tokens for multiple models, you can choose the most cost-effective model for your use case. For example, some models may process Chinese more efficiently (fewer Tokens), some may process code more efficiently.

Practical Use Cases

API Cost Estimation: Before calling LLM APIs, use Token counting tools to estimate input and output Token quantities, then calculate expected costs based on model pricing. This helps budget control and avoids unexpected charges. For example, if you know your prompt is 1000 Tokens and expect 500 Tokens of output, you can estimate the API call cost.

Text Segmentation: When processing long documents (like books, reports, codebases), you need to segment text to ensure each segment is within the model's Context Window. Token counting tools help determine optimal segmentation points, avoiding both too small segments (wasting API calls) and too large segments (exceeding Context Window).

Prompt Engineering: When developing AI applications, you need to repeatedly test and optimize prompts. Token counting tools help you understand the Token consumption of different prompt versions, choose the most cost-effective prompt. For example, you may find that adding examples increases Token consumption but significantly improves output quality, making it worth the cost.

Multi-language Text Processing: Different languages have different Token efficiencies. English typically has the highest Token efficiency (about 0.75 words per Token), Chinese and other logographic languages have lower efficiency (about 0.4-0.6 characters per Token). By counting Tokens, you can better understand the cost of processing multilingual text and choose the appropriate model.

How to Use

Step 1: Enter or paste the text content you want to count in the input box

Step 2: Select the target model (GPT-3.5/GPT-4, Claude, Gemini, Llama, etc.)

Step 3: View Token quantity, character count, word count, and estimated cost

Key Features

Multi-Model Support: Supports Tokenizers for mainstream models including GPT-3.5/GPT-4 (OpenAI), Claude (Anthropic), Gemini (Google), Llama (Meta), and others. Different models use different Tokenization algorithms, so Token quantities may vary for the same text.

Real-Time Statistics: Updates Token quantity in real-time as you type, supports mixed Chinese and English text. No need to click buttons, results display immediately, greatly improving work efficiency.

Cost Estimation: Estimates API call costs based on different models' pricing. Displays input and output costs separately, helping you optimize prompt and choose the most cost-effective model.

Detailed Analysis: Displays not only total Token quantity but also character count, word count, line count, and other statistics. Helps you understand text characteristics from multiple dimensions.

Batch Processing: Supports uploading multiple text files for batch Token counting, automatically generating statistics reports. Suitable for processing large numbers of documents or codebases.

Tokenization Algorithms Explained

BPE (Byte-Pair Encoding): An algorithm that iteratively merges the most frequent character pairs. GPT-2, GPT-3, GPT-4 use BPE variants. BPE achieves a balance between character-level and word-level representations, can handle rare words by decomposing them into subwords, and reduces vocabulary size. For example, "unhappiness" might be tokenized as ["un", "happiness"] or ["un", "hap", "pi", "ness"].

WordPiece: Similar to BPE but uses a different merge strategy, maximizing the likelihood of training data. BERT models use WordPiece. WordPiece tends to prefer longer complete words and splits rare words into more meaningful subwords.

Unigram: A probabilistic model that starts with a large vocabulary and iteratively removes the Token that least reduces the model's likelihood. T5, ALBERT models use Unigram. Unigram can handle multilingual text well and is suitable for models supporting multiple languages.

SentencePiece: A data-driven Tokenization method that treats whitespace as a regular symbol and can train Tokenizers from any language's raw text. It's widely used in multilingual models (like mT5, XLM-R). SentencePiece doesn't require pre-tokenization and can handle any language.

Token Efficiency by Language

Token efficiency varies significantly by language because different Tokenizers are optimized for different languages:

  • English: Highest efficiency, about 0.75 words per Token (about 4 characters per Token). Most LLMs are primarily trained on English, so English Tokenization is most efficient.
  • Chinese, Japanese, Korean: Lower efficiency, about 0.4-0.6 characters per Token. CJK characters are typically encoded as multiple Tokens, especially rare characters.
  • Code: Efficiency varies by programming language and coding style. Code with many English keywords is more efficient, code with many special characters or long identifiers is less efficient.
  • Other European Languages: Efficiency between English and CJK, influenced by language characteristics and model training data.

FAQ

Why do Chinese and English Token quantities differ so much?
Most LLM Tokenizers are optimized for English, treating English words as basic units. Chinese characters need to be encoded as UTF-8 byte sequences first, then Tokenized, so each Chinese character typically becomes 2-3 Tokens. Some models (like Qwen, Baichuan) specifically optimize for Chinese and have higher Chinese Token efficiency.

How to reduce Token usage?
Optimize prompts by removing redundant content, using more concise expressions, avoiding repeated instructions. Use system messages instead of repeating instructions in user messages. For long text, consider summarizing first then processing. Choose models with higher Token efficiency for your language.

What happens if Context Window is exceeded?
Most APIs will return an error, refusing to process the request. Some models may automatically truncate text, only processing the first N Tokens, but this may lose important information. It's recommended to check Token quantity before calling APIs and segment text if necessary.

How to count Tokens in code?
Code Tokenization follows the same principles as text, but code has special syntax (indentation, comments, special characters). Some models (like GPT-4) have been fine-tuned on code and handle code Tokenization better. When counting code Tokens, it's recommended to use model-specific Tokenizers.

Is Token count the same as word count?
No, Token count and word count are different concepts. English word count typically counts whitespace-separated words, while Token count splits words into subwords. For example, "tokenization" might be 1 word but 2-3 Tokens. Chinese typically counts characters, which differs even more from Token count.
json.highlight-export.seo.article:
JSON Syntax Highlight Export - Export JSON with Syntax Highlighting to HTML/RTF

What is JSON Syntax Highlighting?

JSON Syntax Highlighting refers to using different colors, font styles, and background colors to distinguish different element types in JSON, making JSON structure clearer and easier to read. Typical color schemes include: string values in green or brown, numbers in orange or red, boolean values in purple or blue, key names in blue or dark color, null values in gray or italic, control symbols (like {}[]:) in dark or default color. This visual distinction greatly improves JSON readability, especially when dealing with large, nested JSON documents, enabling quick location of key fields, identification of data structures, and discovery of syntax errors.

Syntax highlighting export typically generates HTML or RTF (Rich Text Format) format documents. HTML format uses CSS (Cascading Style Sheets) to define colors and styles, can be directly embedded in web pages or Markdown documents, and highlighting effects automatically display. RTF format is a rich text format developed by Microsoft, can be used in almost all word processing software like Microsoft Word, Google Docs, Apple Pages, WPS Office, maintaining highlighting effects. Compared to plain text JSON, highlighted JSON is easier to understand data hierarchy, identify field types, quickly find specific values, especially suitable for technical documentation, API documentation, teaching materials, presentations, technical blogs, and code comments.

The implementation principle of syntax highlighting is to perform lexical analysis on JSON, identifying different token types (strings, numbers, boolean values, null, key names, control symbols), then assigning different styles to each type. Modern editors and IDEs (like VS Code, Sublime Text, Atom) have built-in powerful syntax highlighting functionality, supporting almost all programming languages and data formats. In web applications, JavaScript libraries (like Prism.js, highlight.js, highlighter.js) can be used to implement syntax highlighting, these libraries provide rich themes and language support.

Why Export Syntax-Highlighted JSON?

Technical Documentation Writing: When writing API documentation, technical specifications, system design documents, you often need to include JSON data examples. Plain text JSON examples are hard to read and don't highlight the structure, especially for complex nested JSON. By exporting JSON with syntax highlighting, documentation becomes more professional and easier to understand. Readers can quickly identify data types, field names, values, and relationships. This is especially important for API documentation developers who need to show request/response examples, configuration file examples, data structure examples, etc.

Presentation Materials: In technical sharing, product demonstrations, training lectures, you often need to display JSON data. Using plain text JSON makes presentations look monotonous and hard to read. By exporting JSON with syntax highlighting and inserting it into PowerPoint, Keynote, Google Slides, presentations become more vivid and professional. Syntax highlighting helps the audience quickly understand data structure and focus on key content. This is especially useful for technical team sharing, client demonstrations, teaching lectures, and other scenarios.

Code Reviews and Debugging: During code reviews, developers often need to examine JSON configuration files or API responses. Plain text JSON is hard to quickly spot issues. By exporting JSON with syntax highlighting, reviewers can more easily find syntax errors, type errors, structural issues. Syntax highlighting also helps understand complex nested JSON structures, improving code review efficiency. This is especially useful for configuration file reviews, API debugging, data validation, and other scenarios.

Teaching and Learning: When teaching JSON format, API usage, data structures, using syntax-highlighted JSON examples helps students better understand knowledge points. Color-coded JSON examples are more vivid and easier to remember than plain text. Teachers can use syntax-highlighted JSON in courseware, tutorials, exercises to improve teaching effectiveness. This is especially suitable for programming teaching, API documentation teaching, data structure teaching, and other scenarios.

How to Use

Step 1: Input or paste JSON data in the input box

Step 2: Select export format (HTML/RTF) and color theme

Step 3: Click the export button, copy the highlighted code or download the file

Key Features

Multiple Color Themes: Supports VS Code Dark, Monokai, GitHub Light, Dracula, Solarized, and other popular themes. Different themes suit different scenarios: dark themes for presentations, light themes for documents, high-contrast themes for teaching materials. You can preview theme effects in real-time and choose the most suitable theme.

Multiple Export Formats: Supports HTML (inline CSS) and RTF formats. HTML format can be directly used in web pages, Markdown documents, email bodies, and supports further customization. RTF format can be directly used in Word, Pages, WPS and other word processing software, maintaining formatting.

Custom Styles: Can adjust font size, line number display, background color, transparency, and other parameters. Supports displaying line numbers,方便定位和引用。Can set whether to display line numbers, line number starting value, line number color, etc.

Format Validation: Before exporting, automatically validates JSON format. If syntax errors are found, displays error location and cause, helping fix issues. Ensures exported JSON is valid and correctly highlighted.

Batch Export: Supports uploading multiple JSON files for batch export, automatically generating highlighted documents. Suitable for processing API documentation, configuration files, example collections, etc.

Color Scheme Design

Good color scheme design should consider readability, aesthetics, and color blindness accessibility:

  • String Values: Usually use green or brown, clearly distinguishing from other types. Should avoid using too bright colors to prevent visual fatigue.
  • Numbers: Usually use orange or red, highlighting numerical values. Should ensure numbers stand out against the background color.
  • Boolean Values: Usually use purple or blue, distinguishing from strings and numbers. Can use different shades for true/false.
  • Key Names: Usually use blue or dark color, distinguishing from string values. Can use bold or italic to emphasize.
  • null Values: Usually use gray or italic, indicating special values. Should ensure null is clearly visible.
  • Control Symbols: Usually use dark or default color, avoiding distracting from data content. Brackets and colons should have appropriate visual weight.

HTML vs RTF Format Comparison

HTML Format: Uses CSS to define styles, can be directly embedded in web pages, Markdown documents, email bodies. Supports responsive design, can adapt to different screen sizes. Can be further customized with JavaScript interaction. Advantages: flexible, searchable, editable. Disadvantages: requires browser or HTML viewer to display.

RTF Format: Uses rich text format, can be used in Word, Pages, WPS and other word processing software. Maintains formatting without additional software. Advantages: good compatibility with word processing software, suitable for printing and document editing. Disadvantages: limited style customization, larger file size, not suitable for web use.

FAQ

Can exported HTML be used directly?
Yes, HTML contains inline CSS, can be directly copied to web pages or Markdown documents. No need for additional CSS files or JavaScript, styles automatically apply. HTML can be further edited and customized as needed.

Which software supports RTF format?
RTF format can be used in Microsoft Word, Google Docs, Apple Pages, WPS Office, LibreOffice Writer and almost all word processing software. Just open the RTF file and you can see highlighted JSON, maintaining formatting.

How to customize colors?
Provide multiple built-in themes, and also support custom colors. You can adjust colors for each element type (strings, numbers, booleans, etc.), choose the most suitable color scheme. Custom color schemes can be saved for future use.

Does export support large files?
Supports exporting large JSON files, but extremely large files may affect browser performance. It's recommended to export files under 10MB. For larger files, consider splitting first then exporting.

Can line numbers be displayed?
Yes, supports displaying line numbers. You can set whether to display line numbers, starting line number, line number color, etc. Line numbers help locate and reference specific content, especially useful in documentation and teaching materials.
json.tree.seo.article:
JSON Tree Viewer - Visualize JSON Structure Online

What is JSON Tree Viewer

A JSON Tree Viewer is a specialized tool for visualizing JSON data in a tree structure. JSON data often contains multi-level nested objects and arrays, making it difficult to intuitively understand hierarchical relationships and structure in plain text format. The tree viewer parses JSON into collapsible tree nodes, where each node represents a JSON element (object, array, string, number, boolean, etc.), clearly showing parent-child relationships through indentation and connector lines.

Tree Structure Example

{
 "user": {
 "id": 1001,
 "name": "John Doe",
 "email": "john@example.com",
 "roles": ["admin", "user"],
 "profile": {
 "age": 28,
 "city": "Beijing",
 "hobbies": ["coding", "reading"]
 }
 }
}

In tree view, the JSON above displays as a collapsible hierarchical structure. Click the arrow icon before a node to expand or collapse child nodes.

Practical Use Cases

  • API Response Analysis: When debugging RESTful APIs, server JSON responses can be very complex with multiple nested data levels. The tree viewer helps quickly locate key fields, verify response data correctness, without manually parsing the entire JSON string.
  • Configuration File Inspection: Application configuration files (like config.json) usually contain multi-level nested configuration items. The tree viewer helps developers quickly browse the configuration structure and find items to modify, avoiding missing important settings.
  • Data Validation: When processing JSON data from external sources, the tree viewer intuitively checks if the data structure meets expectations, verifies required fields exist, and confirms data types are correct.
  • Documentation Writing: When writing technical or API documentation, JSON data structure examples need to be displayed. Tree view clearly presents the data organization, helping readers quickly understand the data model.
  • Data Migration: During data migration or system refactoring, you need to compare JSON data structures between old and new systems. The tree viewer can display two JSON files' tree structures side-by-side, making it easy to identify structural differences.

How to Use - 3 Simple Steps

  1. Step 1: Input JSON - Paste JSON data into the input box or upload a .json file. The tool automatically parses the JSON format.
  2. Step 2: Generate Tree - Click the "Generate Tree View" button. The tool parses the JSON into tree nodes with automatic indentation and grouping.
  3. Step 3: Browse Nodes - Click the triangle icon before nodes to expand/collapse child nodes. Hover over nodes to view detailed information (path, type, value).

Key Features

  • Smart Parsing: Supports standard JSON format, automatically recognizes data types (object, array, string, number, boolean, null), using different icons and colors for different types.
  • Collapsible Nodes: Large JSON tree views can be very deep. Click the triangle icon before a node to collapse all its child nodes, simplifying the view to focus on specific branches.
  • Path Display: Hovering over a node displays its complete path in the JSON (like user.profile.city), making it easy to quickly locate and reference.
  • Type Identification: Uses different colors and icons to distinguish data types (objects use folder icons, arrays use list icons, strings use text icons, etc.), improving readability.
  • Value Preview: For long strings or large arrays, shows a value preview (first 50 characters of string or array length), avoiding excessive screen space usage.
  • Search Highlight: Provides a search box; after entering keywords, all matching nodes in the tree view are highlighted, quickly locating target data.

Detailed Use Cases

Case 1: Analyzing Complex API Responses

Suppose you're developing an e-commerce application and need to analyze a product details API response. The API returns JSON containing product basic info, specifications, user reviews, recommended products, and multiple nested levels.

{
 "code": 200,
 "message": "success",
 "data": {
 "product": {
 "id": "P12345",
 "name": "Wireless Mechanical Keyboard",
 "price": 599.00,
 "category": {
 "id": 101,
 "name": "Digital Accessories"
 },
 "specifications": {
 "connectivity": ["wired", "wireless", "bluetooth"],
 "layout": "87-key",
 "backlight": "RGB"
 },
 "reviews": {
 "total": 128,
 "average": 4.5,
 "items": [
 {"user": "Alice", "rating": 5, "comment": "Great feel"},
 {"user": "Bob", "rating": 4, "comment": "Good value"}
 ]
 }
 }
 }
}

Using the tree viewer, you can:

  1. Expand data.product node to view product basic information
  2. Expand specifications node to view specifications
  3. Expand reviews.items array to view user reviews
  4. Click any node to view its complete path in the JSON

Case 2: Checking Configuration File Structure

The application's configuration file app.json contains database connection, server settings, logging configuration, and other modules. The tree viewer helps you:

{
 "app": {
 "name": "MyApp",
 "version": "2.0.0",
 "database": {
 "host": "localhost",
 "port": 5432,
 "name": "mydb",
 "pool": {
 "min": 5,
 "max": 20
 }
 },
 "server": {
 "port": 8080,
 "ssl": {
 "enabled": true,
 "cert": "/path/to/cert.pem"
 }
 }
 }
}

In tree view:

  • All configurations are grouped by module (database, server, etc.)
  • You can expand a specific module to view its configuration items
  • Hovering shows the complete path, like app.server.ssl.cert
  • Type identification helps you quickly identify strings, numbers, booleans, etc.

Case 3: Comparing Data Structure Changes

When refactoring an API and changing the response structure from flat to nested, you can use the tree viewer to compare old and new structures:

// Old version (flat structure)
{
 "user_id": 1001,
 "user_name": "John Doe",
 "user_email": "john@example.com",
 "user_age": 28
}

// New version (nested structure)
{
 "user": {
 "id": 1001,
 "name": "John Doe",
 "contact": {
 "email": "john@example.com"
 },
 "info": {
 "age": 28
 }
 }
}

The tree view clearly shows the structural differences, helping frontend developers adjust data access code.

Advanced Features

Large Dataset Processing

The tool supports processing large JSON files (up to 50MB), using incremental rendering. Even with hundreds of thousands of nodes, interaction remains smooth. Nodes load on-demand--only expanded nodes render their children, reducing memory usage.

Export Functionality

Tree views can be exported as images (PNG, JPG) or HTML format, convenient for use in documents and presentations. Exported images preserve the complete tree structure and expansion state.

Comparison Mode

Supports side-by-side display of two JSON files' tree views, with differences highlighted. This is very useful for comparing API version changes, configuration file differences, and more.

Frequently Asked Questions

Q1: What's the maximum JSON size supported?

A: The tool supports JSON files up to 50MB. For larger files, recommend splitting first or simplifying the data structure. The tree view uses on-demand loading, remaining smooth even with hundreds of thousands of nodes.

Q2: How do I copy a specific node's value?

A: Clicking a node selects it, then you can click "Copy Value". For array or object nodes, you can choose to copy as JSON string or only the node path. Copied content is automatically formatted for easy pasting into code.

Q3: Can tree view handle malformed JSON?

A: The tool has a powerful JSON parser that can automatically fix some common formatting errors (missing commas, extra commas). If JSON has severe syntax errors, it displays the specific error location and repair suggestions.

Q4: Can I search for specific nodes?

A: Yes. The tool provides search functionality. Enter a node name or part of a value, and all matching nodes are highlighted. Search supports regular expressions for precise matching of complex patterns.

Q5: Can tree view display comments in JSON?

A: Standard JSON doesn't support comments. However, if your JSON is JSONC format (supports // and /* */ comments), the tool removes comments before displaying the tree structure. Comment content won't appear in the tree view.

Start Using Now

Whether you need to analyze API responses, inspect configuration files, verify data structures, or compare JSON differences, the JSON tree viewer helps you complete tasks quickly. The tool is completely free, no registration required, supports large files, all processing happens locally in your browser ensuring your data security.

CTA Buttons: "View Now" / "Upload File"

JSON Table Viewer - Convert JSON Arrays to Tables Online

What is JSON Table Viewer

A JSON Table Viewer is a tool that converts JSON array data into an intuitive table format. JSON arrays typically contain multiple objects with similar structures (like user lists, product lists, order records, etc.), which are difficult to compare and analyze in plain text format. The table viewer parses JSON arrays into row-column structured tables, where each object corresponds to a row and object keys serve as column headers, making data easier to read, filter, and export.

JSON to Table Example

[
 {
 "id": 1,
 "name": "John Doe",
 "email": "john@example.com",
 "age": 28,
 "department": "Engineering"
 },
 {
 "id": 2,
 "name": "Jane Smith",
 "email": "jane@example.com",
 "age": 32,
 "department": "Marketing"
 },
 {
 "id": 3,
 "name": "Bob Johnson",
 "email": "bob@example.com",
 "age": 25,
 "department": "Design"
 }
]

When converted to a table, data displays in row-column format, allowing intuitive comparison of each person's information, making it easy to read and understand.

Practical Use Cases

  • Data Report Analysis: User data, order data, and sales data exported from business systems are usually in JSON array format. Using the table viewer, you can quickly convert data to table format for data summarization, trend analysis, and outlier detection.
  • API Result Browsing: List-type APIs (like fetching user lists, product lists) typically return JSON arrays. The table viewer displays results in table format, convenient for browsing, sorting, and filtering data.
  • Data Cleaning: In data processing workflows, you need to check data quality in JSON arrays. Table view can quickly identify missing values, formatting errors, duplicate data, etc., facilitating data cleaning.
  • CSV Conversion Preparation: When importing JSON data into Excel or other data analysis tools, first use the table viewer to preview data structure, confirm column names and data types are correct before conversion.
  • Configuration Comparison: Multiple environment configuration files (like dev, test, production environment configs) can be displayed side-by-side in table format, convenient for comparing configuration differences between environments.

How to Use - 3 Steps to Generate Table

  1. Step 1: Input JSON Array - Paste JSON array data into the input box or upload a .json file. The tool automatically detects if it's an array format.
  2. Step 2: Generate Table - Click the "Generate Table" button. The tool parses the JSON array, extracts all object keys as column headers, and each object as a row of data.
  3. Step 3: Browse and Operate - Use the search box at the top of the table to filter data, click column headers to sort, select needed rows to copy or export.

Key Features

  • Automatic Column Detection: The tool scans all objects in the JSON array, extracting all keys that appear as table columns. Even if some objects are missing certain fields, the table correctly displays them as empty cells.
  • Smart Type Recognition: Automatically recognizes field data types (string, number, boolean, date, etc.), right-aligns numeric columns, formats date columns for display, improving readability.
  • Sorting Function: Click any column header to sort the table in ascending or descending order. Supports sorting by numeric value, alphabetical order, and date sequence.
  • Search Filter: Provides a global search box; after entering keywords, the table automatically filters to show only rows containing that keyword. Search supports fuzzy matching and regular expressions.
  • Pagination Display: For JSON arrays containing large amounts of data, the table supports pagination display. You can set the number of rows per page (10/50/100/500 rows), avoiding page lag.
  • Column Display Control: You can choose to show or hide specific columns. When JSON objects contain many fields, you can focus only on needed columns, simplifying the view.

Using the table viewer, you can:

Case 2: Viewing E-commerce Order Lists

Advanced Features

Nested Object Flattening

When JSON objects contain nested objects, the table viewer automatically expands nested fields into multi-level column names. For example, customer.name expands as an independent column, convenient for viewing and filtering.

Array Field Processing

For fields containing arrays (like product lists in orders), the table viewer displays the array length. Clicking the cell can expand to view array contents.

Data Export

Column Width Adjustment

You can drag column boundaries to adjust column width. Double-clicking column boundaries automatically adjusts column width to fit content width.

A: The tool supports JSON files up to 10MB, approximately 100,000 rows. For larger datasets, recommend data filtering or pagination first. The table uses virtual scrolling technology, remaining smooth even with large amounts of data.

Q2: How to handle inconsistent object fields in JSON arrays?

A: The tool automatically collects all keys that appear in objects as column names. If an object is missing a field, the corresponding cell displays as empty. This ensures table structure integrity without data misalignment due to missing fields.

Q3: Can I view multiple JSON arrays simultaneously?

A: The current version only supports viewing one JSON array at a time. To compare multiple arrays, recommend merging them into one array, adding a "source" field to each object to identify the source, then using filter functionality to view separately.

Q4: Can table data be copied to Excel?

A: Yes. After selecting rows in the table, click the "Copy" button. Data copies to the clipboard in tab-separated format and can be directly pasted into Excel. You can also use the export CSV function to directly generate Excel-openable files.

Q5: How to save table view state (like sorting, filtering)?

A: The tool automatically saves view state to browser local storage. After refreshing the page, sorting and filter status automatically restores. If you need to share a specific view, you can use the URL parameter feature to encode the current view state into the URL to share with others.

Start Using Now

Whether you need to analyze user data, browse API results, clean data quality, or compare configuration differences, the JSON table viewer helps you complete tasks quickly. The tool is completely free, no registration required, supports large datasets, all processing happens locally in your browser ensuring your data security.

json.diff.seo.article:
JSON Diff Tool - Compare Two JSON Files Online

What is JSON Diff Tool

A JSON Diff Tool is a specialized tool for comparing differences between two JSON documents. In software development and data processing, you often need to compare different versions of configuration files, API responses, data structures, etc. The JSON diff tool intelligently parses two JSON files, identifies added, deleted, and modified fields, and displays difference locations and specific change contents in an intuitive way.

JSON Diff Example

Suppose you have two JSON files to compare:

// Original version (old.json)
{
 "user": {
 "id": 1001,
 "name": "John Doe",
 "email": "john@example.com",
 "age": 28,
 "address": {
 "city": "Beijing",
 "district": "Chaoyang"
 }
 },
 "status": "active"
}

// New version (new.json)
{
 "user": {
 "id": 1001,
 "name": "John Doe",
 "email": "john@newdomain.com",
 "age": 29,
 "address": {
 "city": "Beijing",
 "district": "Haidian",
 "zipCode": "100086"
 },
 "phone": "138****1234"
 },
 "status": "active",
 "role": "admin"
}

The comparison results clearly show:

  • user.email: "john@example.com" → "john@newdomain.com" (modified)
  • user.age: 28 → 29 (modified)
  • user.address.district: "Chaoyang" → "Haidian" (modified)
  • user.address.zipCode: "100086" (added)
  • user.phone: "138****1234" (added)
  • role: "admin" (added)

Practical Use Cases

  • Configuration File Version Comparison: During system upgrades, configuration files may change. Using the diff tool, you can quickly identify differences between old and new configurations, ensuring proper system operation after upgrades.
  • API Response Validation: During API development, you need to verify that modified API responses meet expectations. Comparing response data before and after modifications can quickly reveal unexpected changes.
  • Data Migration Validation: When performing data migration, you need to compare data before and after migration to ensure data integrity and consistency. The diff tool can quickly discover data loss or errors.
  • Code Review: In team development, when reviewing others' code modifications, the diff tool helps understand data structure changes and assess the impact scope of modifications.
  • Debugging Issues: When system issues occur, comparing data differences between normal and abnormal states can quickly locate the root cause.

How to Use - 3 Steps to Compare JSON

  1. Step 1: Input Original JSON - Paste the original JSON data into the left input box or upload the original .json file.
  2. Step 2: Input New JSON - Paste the new JSON data into the right input box or upload the new .json file.
  3. Step 3: View Differences - Click the "Compare" button. The tool automatically analyzes differences between the two JSONs and displays changes with highlighting.

Key Features

  • Smart Difference Detection: The tool recursively compares each field of the JSON, identifying added, deleted, and modified values. For nested objects and arrays, it accurately identifies differences within internal elements.
  • Visual Difference Display: Uses color coding to distinguish different types of differences: red for modifications, green for additions, gray for deletions. Side-by-side view allows direct comparison of two JSONs.
  • Path Location: Clicking any difference item displays its complete path in the JSON (like user.address.city), making quick positioning easy.
  • Array Element Comparison: For array types, the tool compares each element by index. Even with long arrays, it accurately identifies which elements have changed.
  • Difference Statistics: Displays total difference statistics, including number of modified fields, added fields, and deleted fields, helping quickly understand the scale of changes.
  • Ignore Order: Provides an option to ignore array element order, comparing only content. This is very useful for scenarios where order doesn't matter.

Detailed Use Cases

Case 1: API Version Upgrade Comparison

Suppose you're upgrading the user API from v1 to v2 and need to compare response differences between the old and new versions.

// API v1 response
{
 "code": 200,
 "message": "success",
 "data": {
 "id": 1001,
 "username": "zhangsan",
 "email": "zhangsan@example.com",
 "created_at": "2020-01-15T08:30:00Z"
 }
}

// API v2 response
{
 "code": 200,
 "message": "success",
 "data": {
 "id": 1001,
 "username": "zhangsan",
 "email": "zhangsan@example.com",
 "firstName": "Zhang",
 "lastName": "San",
 "profile": {
 "avatar": "https://example.com/avatar.jpg",
 "bio": "Full Stack Developer"
 },
 "createdAt": "2020-01-15T08:30:00Z"
 }
}

Comparison results reveal:

  1. Added firstName and lastName fields (username split)
  2. Added profile nested object containing avatar and bio
  3. created_at changed to createdAt (naming style change)

These changes help frontend developers adjust data binding logic to ensure proper functionality after upgrades.

Case 2: Configuration File Migration Validation

During microservice architecture migration, you need to compare the old monolithic application configuration with the new microservice configuration.

// Old configuration (monolith.json)
{
 "app": {
 "name": "MyApp",
 "port": 8080,
 "database": {
 "host": "localhost",
 "port": 5432,
 "name": "mydb"
 }
 },
 "features": {
 "cache": true,
 "search": true
 }
}

// New configuration (user-service.json)
{
 "app": {
 "name": "user-service",
 "port": 8081
 },
 "database": {
 "host": "db.cluster.local",
 "port": 5432,
 "name": "users_db",
 "pool": {
 "min": 5,
 "max": 20
 }
 },
 "features": {
 "cache": true,
 "cache": {
 "type": "redis",
 "ttl": 3600
 }
 }
}

Comparison results show:

  • Database name changed from "mydb" to "users_db"
  • Database address changed from localhost to cluster address
  • Added database connection pool configuration
  • Cache configuration changed from boolean to object, including type and TTL
  • Removed search feature (likely handled by another service)

Case 3: Data Backup Recovery Validation

After database backup and recovery, compare original data with recovered data to ensure data integrity.

// Original data export
[
 {"id": 1, "name": "Product A", "price": 99.99, "stock": 100},
 {"id": 2, "name": "Product B", "price": 149.99, "stock": 50},
 {"id": 3, "name": "Product C", "price": 199.99, "stock": 75}
]

// Recovered data
[
 {"id": 1, "name": "Product A", "price": 99.99, "stock": 100},
 {"id": 2, "name": "Product B", "price": 149.99, "stock": 48},
 {"id": 3, "name": "Product C", "price": 199.99, "stock": 75},
 {"id": 4, "name": "Product D", "price": 299.99, "stock": 25}
]

Comparison reveals:

  • Product B stock changed from 50 to 48 (transactions occurred during recovery)
  • Added Product D (new data added during recovery)

These differences are acceptable, indicating the backup recovery process was normal.

Advanced Features

Difference Export

Comparison results can be exported as JSON format difference reports, containing detailed descriptions of all changes. This is very useful for documentation and team sharing.

Batch Comparison

Supports batch comparison of multiple JSON files, generating difference summary reports. Suitable for configuration file batch migration scenarios.

Custom Comparison Rules

You can set rules to ignore differences in specific fields, such as timestamps, IDs, and other frequently changing values. You can also set tolerance ranges for numeric comparisons to avoid false positives from floating-point precision issues.

JSON Patch Generation

Automatically generates RFC 6902 standard JSON Patch documents based on differences, which can be used to programmatically apply changes.

Frequently Asked Questions

Q1: What's the maximum file size the JSON diff tool supports?

A: The tool supports JSON files up to 10MB. For larger files, recommend filtering data first or using professional JSON processing tools.

Q2: How to handle different array orders?

A: The tool provides an "Ignore Array Order" option. When enabled, the tool compares array content rather than order, suitable for arrays where order doesn't matter but content does.

Q3: Can comparison results be saved?

A: Yes. You can export difference reports as JSON files or copy difference summaries to clipboard. Exported reports contain detailed information about all changes and can be used for documentation or team sharing.

Q4: Can the tool handle malformed JSON?

A: The tool automatically attempts to fix common JSON format issues (missing quotes, extra commas). If JSON has severe errors, it displays the specific error location and suggestions.

Q5: Can I ignore certain fields when comparing?

A: Yes. You can set ignore rules specifying field paths to ignore. For example, ignore all fields containing "timestamp" to avoid timestamp differences interfering with comparison results.

Whether you need to compare configuration file versions, validate API response changes, check data migration results, or review code modifications, the JSON diff tool helps you complete tasks quickly. The tool is completely free, no registration required, supports large files, all processing happens locally in your browser ensuring your data security.

json.minify.seo.article:
JSON Minify Tool - Online JSON Compressor

What is JSON Minify Tool

The JSON Minify Tool is a professional online utility designed to convert formatted JSON data into a compact, single-line format. By removing all unnecessary whitespace, line breaks, and indentation, the JSON Minify Tool can significantly reduce JSON file sizes by 30-60%, which is crucial for network transmission, storage optimization, and improving load times.

Core features include:

  • Removing all whitespace characters (spaces, tabs, line breaks)
  • Eliminating unnecessary separators in objects and arrays
  • Maintaining JSON structure integrity and validity
  • Real-time preview of compression results and ratios
  • Support for rapid processing of large JSON files

Practical Use Cases

Web Application Optimization

  • Reduce API response data transfer
  • Optimize frontend configuration file sizes
  • Improve mobile loading speeds
  • Lower bandwidth consumption and server costs

Data Storage Optimization

  • Reduce NoSQL database storage space
  • Optimize log file storage
  • Compress configuration files and user data

  • Reduce backup file sizes
  • Improve data processing efficiency

How to Use

Step 1: Input JSON Data

Paste your formatted JSON data into the left input box, or click the "Upload File" button to select a local JSON file. The tool supports drag-and-drop upload and can handle files up to 50MB.

Step 2: Execute Compression

Click the "Minify JSON" button, and the tool will immediately process your data. The compression process is completed entirely in your browser locally - your data is never uploaded to any server, ensuring 100% data security.

Step 3: Copy or Download Results

After compression is complete, the right side will display the minimized JSON data. You can:

  • Click "Copy Result" to copy the compressed JSON to clipboard
  • Click "Download File" to save the result as a .json file
  • View compression statistics (original size, compressed size, compression ratio)

Key Features

Smart Compression Algorithm

  • Automatically identifies and removes all unnecessary whitespace
  • Preserves necessary spaces within strings
  • Maintains JSON structure integrity
  • Supports nested objects and arrays

Real-time Preview

  • Instant display of compression results
  • Shows size comparison before and after compression
  • Calculates compression ratio percentage
  • Character count statistics

Batch Processing

  • Support for simultaneous multi-file compression
  • Automatically process all JSON files in a folder
  • Batch download compressed results
  • Progress bar shows processing status

Secure and Reliable

  • 100% client-side processing, no data upload
  • Supports HTTPS encrypted transmission
  • Does not retain any user data
  • Complies with GDPR and CCPA privacy regulations

Flexible Input/Output

  • Supports text paste and file upload
  • Drag-and-drop upload support
  • URL parameter import (supports CORS)
  • Multiple download format options

Advanced Options

  • Compression options to preserve partial formatting
  • Unicode escape control
  • Trailing comma handling
  • Comment preservation options

Detailed Use Cases

Case 1: Optimizing API Response Data

Suppose you have a user information API that returns the following JSON data:


{
"users": [
{
"id": 1001,
"name": "John Doe",
"email": "john@example.com",
"age": 28,
"city": "New York",

},
{

"age": 32,
"city": "Los Angeles",
"status": "active"
}
],
"total": 2,
"page": 1
}

Original size: 348 bytes

After compression:


{"users":[{"id":1001,"name":"John Doe","email":"john@example.com","age":28,"city":"New York","status":"active"},{"id":1002,"name":"Jane Smith","email":"jane@example.com","age":32,"city":"Los Angeles","status":"active"}],"total":2,"page":1}

Compressed size: 235 bytes, compression ratio: 32%

ROI Analysis:

  • If you have 1 million API requests per day
  • Each request saves 113 bytes
  • Can save approximately 113MB of bandwidth per day
  • Can save approximately 3.4GB of traffic costs per month

Case 2: Optimizing Frontend Configuration Files

Large single-page applications typically contain extensive configuration data:


{
"app": {
"name": "Enterprise Management System",

},

Case 3: Optimizing Internationalization Language Packs

}

After compression, the language pack file size can be reduced from 2.3KB to 1.4KB, which is particularly important for mobile applications.

Advanced Features

Preserve Key Comments

  • Support for preserving important comments in JSON
  • Intelligent identification of documentation comments
  • Custom comment marker formats

Selective Compression

  • Specify certain keys not to be compressed
  • Preserve formatting for specific paths
  • Whitelist/blacklist mode

  • Standard compression (balanced mode)
  • Extreme compression (minimum size)

Batch Optimization

  • Command-line interface support
  • CI/CD integration capability
  • Automated build scripts

Performance Analysis

  • Compression time statistics
  • Memory usage monitoring
  • Large file processing optimization

Format Conversion

  • Simultaneous support for compression and formatting
  • YAML/JSON mutual conversion compression
  • XML to JSON compression

FAQ

Q1: Can compressed JSON be restored to its original format?

A: Yes, JSON compression only removes whitespace characters, and the data structure is completely preserved. You can use our JSON Pretty Print tool to reformat the compressed JSON into a readable form. Compression and formatting are reversible operations that do not lose any data.

Q2: Will the compressed JSON file be corrupted?

A: Not at all. Our compression tool only removes whitespace characters (spaces, newlines, tabs) and does not modify any actual data. The compressed JSON still conforms to JSON specifications and can be correctly parsed by any standard JSON parser. The tool also includes built-in validation to ensure the correctness of compression results.

Q3: Why does some JSON show no size change after compression?

A: This usually happens in the following cases:

  • The JSON data is already in compressed format
  • The data is mainly string content with a small proportion of whitespace
  • The JSON structure is simple with few nesting levels

For such data, we recommend checking if it has already been compressed, or consider using general compression algorithms like gzip for secondary compression.

Q4: Can I compress JSON containing special characters?

A: Fully supported. Our tool correctly handles all JSON special characters, including:

  • Unicode characters (Chinese, Japanese, Korean, etc.)
  • Escape characters (\n, \t, \", \\, etc.)
  • Special symbols (@, #, $, %, etc.)
  • Emoji emoticons

The tool intelligently identifies spaces inside and outside strings, only removing safe whitespace characters.

Q5: How long does it take to compress large JSON files?

A: Processing speed depends on file size and browser performance:

  • Less than 1MB: Almost instant (< 100ms)
  • 1-10MB: Usually completes within 1-3 seconds
  • 10-50MB: Approximately 5-15 seconds
  • Larger files: Recommend using command-line tools

Our tool uses optimized algorithms to efficiently handle large files. For very large files, we recommend validating JSON format correctness first to avoid wasting time processing erroneous data.
